frameworks.name: channel encoding model
frameworks.one_line: population BOLD response as linear sum of orientation-tuned channels

frameworks.name: inverted encoding model (IEM)
frameworks.one_line: inversion used to claim inferences about single-unit tuning width

frameworks.name: signal-detection / SNR framework
frameworks.one_line: channel function shape is driven by population SNR rather than tuning width

core_question: Can inverted encoding models recover single-neuron tuning width from human BOLD population responses?
key_assumptions: population response is linear sum of channel responses
orientation tuning of single units is contrast-invariant in V1
BOLD measurements reflect aggregated channel activity with noise
manipulations: simulated signal-to-noise ratio of population response
key_findings: inverted channel response functions broadened at lower contrast in human V1
broadening reproduced in simulations with contrast-invariant single-unit tuning plus reduced amplitude
higher SNR makes recovered channel functions insensitive to true neural tuning width
in zero-noise limit, IEM returns the assumed channel function regardless of single-unit bandwidth
interpretations: V1 data are consistent with contrast-invariant orientation tuning despite apparent IEM broadening
IEM-derived population selectivity conflates neural tuning width with noise and is better used to index decoding uncertainty
limitations_noted: inferences about tuning width from IEMs are confounded by SNR
